findings.title: updatePrStatus failures (incl. new timeout) silently swallowed, no error shown
findings.why_it_matters: markStatus's catch block only calls load() again and relies on a stale comment ('Error is surfaced by load()') that is incorrect: load() unconditionally resets error.value to null at its top and only re-sets it if the *subsequent* /api/pr-queue GET also fails. A failure specific to the status update itself (server rejects the transition, non-2xx response, or -- newly, after this diff -- an AbortError from _fetchWithTimeout's 15s timeout) is discarded entirely. The row's spinner just disappears with no role=alert error and no visible change, so the user cannot tell their 'mark as won/skip' click failed versus succeeded. This existed before the diff (e.g. the already-tested `ok: false, error: 'invalid transition'` case in prQueue.spec.ts already hit this same swallow), but B3's new bounded timeout turns what used to be an infinite hang (at least visibly 'stuck') into a fast, completely silent no-op, undercutting the stated goal of giving the user 'a way out' of a failed action.
findings.suggested_fix: In markStatus's catch block, surface the update failure (e.g. set error.value = e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e)) before calling load(), or show a dedicated per-row/toast message) instead of relying on load() to coincidentally re-surface an unrelated fetch failure.
evidence:
frontend/src/pages/PrQueue/PrQueuePage.vue:105-116: `try { await updatePrStatus(id, status); await load() } catch { // Error is surfaced by load()
 await load() } finally { ... }` -- the comment is inaccurate: load() (lines 64-100) resets error.value = null unconditionally before doing any work, so it can only surface an error from its own /app-config+/api/pr-queue calls, never from the updatePrStatus call that actually failed.

StateBlock.vue:59 only renders `state === 'error'` (role=alert) when PrQueuePage's `error` ref is truthy; PrQueuePage.vue's blockState computed (lines 39-45) derives 'error' solely from `error.value`, which markStatus's catch never sets.

prQueue.spec.ts's own test 'throws the server-provided error message when ok: false' proves updatePrStatus can reject with a meaningful message ('invalid transition') that, per the above, never reaches the UI when triggered via markStatus.

frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts's new _fetchWithTimeout (lines 35-39) means updatePrStatus can now also reject with an AbortError after 15s, adding a new common trigger for the same pre-existing silent-swallow path.

residual_risks: Worst-case latency for updatePrStatus is now bounded but can still reach ~30s (15s for the _csrf() CSRF-token fetch timeout, plus 15s for the status-update fetch timeout) when no <meta name="csrf-token"> tag is present and the token endpoint itself stalls -- still much better than an infinite hang, but the UI gives no distinct 'this is taking a while' feedback during that window beyond the existing button spinner.

The request-generation guard in PrQueuePage.vue's load() (frontend/src/pages/PrQueue/PrQueuePage.vue:65-99) is logically sound for arbitrary interleavings (monotonic counter compared at three checkpoints), but its correctness for the specific 'older-started request resolves after a newer one already rendered fresh data' scenario described in the B4 intent is inferred from static tracing rather than being directly exercised by an automated test (see testing_gaps).
testing_gaps: The new PrQueuePage.spec.ts test ('a load() superseded by a newer one never touches the network or shared state') only exercises the first generation checkpoint (frontend/src/pages/PrQueue/PrQueuePage.vue:83, 'superseded while checking /app-config') -- traced execution shows the superseded call's own config-check resolves before the fetchPrQueue call is ever issued, so the second checkpoint at line 90 ('superseded while fetching the queue', the closer analogue to B4's stated bug of a slow response overwriting a fast one) is never actually hit by this test.

prQueue.spec.ts's timeout test ('propagates an AbortError instead of hanging when the request times out') mocks fetch to reject with an AbortError immediately rather than using fake timers to let _fetchWithTimeout's own setTimeout/AbortController actually fire at 15s -- the real timer-driven abort path in frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts (lines 35-39) is not exercised end-to-end.

No automated test covers the user-facing outcome when updatePrStatus rejects (timeout, HTTP error, or ok:false) via markStatus in PrQueuePage.vue -- only the underlying API function's rejection is tested in isolation (prQueue.spec.ts), not what the page displays (or fails to display) to the user afterward.

webui_app/static/js/index.js's B5 fix is verified only by a literal-string-count assertion (tests/test_webui_feedback_states.py's test_health_bar_fetch_resolution_rechecks_dismiss_before_showing) rather than a runtime/jsdom test that actually delays the /health fetch and asserts the bar stays hidden after a dismiss -- consistent with this file's existing test style, but it would not catch a change that duplicates the string in a semantically wrong location.
